title: 1,000 Miles On My Legs Bike Touring Vlog - Bike Touring Through Europe 5-2010

transcript.text: Hello. I'm going to do bike journal, thousand mile mark. I think it'd be cool to journal, make a journal of how I feel physically as my mileage on my legs goes. Just to share what my experience is as far as getting into shape. I pretty much started from scratch. I didn't do anything at all pretty much for like three months or something. Before that I was in shape, but it was in weightlifting shape.

And I wasn't really, I wasn't fit as in low, low fat. And I'm actually really, I'm still like high fat right now. Big old belly, like barely smaller than what I was. And, uh, you know, I'm not totally fit now. Um, I have 977 miles on my odometer. So that's, um, 777 miles, um, on this trip. Cause I had 200 miles on my legs and a boulder, um, when I was there for the month of, um,

March. Um, and I'm starting to feel like I'm, um, my leg speed is starting to come into and coming in, you know, like, like say if I'm fresh in the next couple of days and somebody like zooms by me and they're going really fast, like I could like get on that wheel or something, you know, like, like I feel like my leg speed is a lot faster than it was like when I was in Boulder.

Like when I was in Boulder, I wasn't fast. Like all the girls are faster than me, but now I'm probably faster than all the girls. I don't know. Um, my legs are still not in good shape. Um, I'm having asthma, you know, I'm up at two hours every single night, um, with asthma, not being able to breathe. Um, so then I have to, from like three to five.

So then I have to sleep from like five to eight. Like I have to catch up on the sleep cause I don't get to sleep in those hours, but that could be cause I'm in the tent, you know, and the carbon monoxide or something. But, um, but writing, I don't really have asthma, but I am kind of still getting, I'm still hacking up a lot of yellow phlegm and I'm having my asthma while I'm writing sometimes.

Well, I can feel, you know, my lungs are never totally cleaner. They're still a little bit asthmatic, but hopefully after 2000, um, I'll, uh, my lungs problems will be gone. Hopefully. Cause if they don't go, then I'm, I'm like, I'm fucked. I'm not gonna, I'm not ever gonna get like strong, strong and fuck. Maybe I'll be able to die.

Um, and I haven't dropped the, dropped the belly yet. So maybe after 2000, I will have dropped the belly. I am kind of eating a lot still. Um, but a couple of days ago, uh, what was it? Like three days ago, Southern Germany, I felt like I had leg speed for the first time. And that was cool. Like I was moving, you know, I was in the drops for the first day. I was right on the road for the first day. And on the flats, I was averaging like 20,

22, 23, 24, 25 miles an hour, you know, like no slower than 22. I think the wind was with me though. Cause I was looking at the grass and it seemed like the grass was being pushed on that day. And then the next day I was totally wasted. Um, and then the day after that, which would have been, I guess it was yesterday that I was totally wasted.

Yeah. Yeah. Yesterday I was totally wasted. And then today I felt pretty good, but I'm totally wasted now. Like fever. I'm totally fever now. Um, so tomorrow, and my legs are kind of burning. So tomorrow, I kind of have an off day tomorrow because I'll be hiking through the snow and it's just different muscles and stuff.

Um, and then I'll be going downhill. So, um, basically my strategy is until is, um, originally my strategy, my prediction, which I made in the video when I was tripping on mushrooms. It didn't get, then I didn't upload because I recorded on my, um, my, my, my, my, my tape. I predicted that after three or four weeks, I would be like strong, like strong rider. Cause that's, that's what I remember it being when I was in Australia.

And I was, you know, started pretty much started from scratch there. So right now it's the 24th and I started on the seventh or I started by turn pretty much like on the ninth, but you know, I had like 200 miles before that. Um, so I'm coming up on three weeks, you know, so I'm starting to get on the, the, um, leg speed and stuff. Um, so yeah, but as far as like training, you know, like overtraining or whatever, or like riding hard to get,

Get strong or whatever, I'm not going to worry about any of that yet. I mean, I can't anyway, because if I ride too hard and then my heart rate gets up above like 140 or 150, I'll get an asthma attack. So I basically, I'm basically a slave to however my lungs are going. So, um, so I'm hoping after another thousand miles, you know, a couple of weeks or whatever, I'll, um, I'll, uh, the asthma will go away. Cause it, cause my asthma has been gone away before.

Yeah. So, um, yeah. So, you know, I, I have like hammering days and I have like easy, easy days where I just try to go easy and I've only had really one of those, but you know, I'm kind of, this is kind of how I am right now. I'm just like, I just ride, you know, and if I feel like riding hard, I'll ride hard. But I'm not worried. I'm not sweating it back in a long time ago. You know, when I was like 22, I did a bike tour through America, you know, state Colorado.

Utah and Arizona and stuff. I would start the bike tour at the very beginning riding as hard as I possibly could, you know, and then I would, so after three weeks, I'd be like super strong, but mentally I'm not there. You know, my lungs can't handle it now.

Um, I'm bigger now. So my heart has to pump more blood through my body. You know, I have more fat on my body. I'm older. It's just different, but it's good because, you know, maybe 10 years ago I'd start bike tour and I'd burn myself out mentally, you know, cause you want to have fun.

Um, and you know, I think if my legs are, if I'm shot, you know, my legs are sore, that's good enough. You know, I'm hoping I'd like to get to the point where I can, no matter how hard I ride every day, my legs are never going to be sore.

Pretty much, you know, unless I do like a 24 hour ride. So anyways, tomorrow I'm going to go into Leichenstein and check it out, you know, add that other country on my list. And then I'm going to go into Austria, which will be lots of hills.

So that'll be good exercise for me. Um, you know, and if the hills are really steep, then my God, it'll be a really good exercise. Cause I don't, I have a, I'm on a race bike. I don't have like a small chain ring, you know, and I'm touring.

So it's not that easy for me to go up one of these beyond grade hills, especially if it's like the end of a 80 mile day, you know? Um, so anyways, we'll see. I'm looking forward to riding through the mountains though. It looks like the mountains are going to be beautiful.

So anyways, predictions for after 2000 miles, um, are that I think I'll be strong. I'm going to be in, uh, poor countries like, um, Poland and Croatia and Greece and stuff that aren't going to have bypass.
So I'm going to be on the road with the cars, which is going to be good because I can get into a rhythm and I can just concentrate on riding.
I don't have to start and stop and get lost and go in circles because then you start again and your legs are burning, you know, and you don't want to hammer again.
But when you ride on a road, you know, and it's like 50 kilometers to the next town or, you know, cause I have four water bottles.

So it's like, I don't have to stop for hours. I can get into rhythm and I can, I can, you know, the way that bike riding works, if you're not a bike rider, is that, is that you, you, you, you punch your level up a little bit incrementally as you're riding.
You know, it's like you're doing 19 miles an hour and then you pump it up to 20 and then you pump it up to 21 and then you pump it up to 22 and then you pump it up to 23.

Next thing you know, you're like heart rate, you're breathing hard and your heart rate's pounding and you're sweating like a pig and you're drinking a lot of water and your legs are burning, but you're going fast.
So you're happy and you just try to hold that as long as you can and, you know, and you can kind of get lost in the rhythm and I'm looking forward to that.

So after a month, you know, I'll be riding in roads like that. I'll probably be, I'll feel like I'm strong, especially if I have a day where I'm like riding with the wind, you know, where I can like average like 25 miles an hour, you know.
But even if I'm against the wind, I can hammer and feel like I'm strong because I remember I was in Australia and I was going like six miles an hour against these like just gale force winds, man.
But I knew I was riding really strong. I mean, I, you know, I killed my chain, but I guess it was an old chain, but so I don't need the wind to go with me to make me feel like I'm strong.

But anyways, after a month, so I'm just curious. I mean, I've never ridden for five months straight before. So theoretically, I'll be in the shape of my life if my lungs can kick into gear, which I think they will.

So I'll be in the shape of my life after five months. I don't really have a, I don't really know what to predict after, you know, a couple thousand miles, but after a thousand, you know, I should be stronger.

And then after 3000, maybe even stronger. And I haven't gotten to a point where I get sick though. I've been like borderline, you know, like where I'm now, but not like actually sick where I have to take a whole day off and just like lay in bed.

Like that happened to me when I was in Morocco. Cause I think, cause I was writing too hard, but, um, but anyways, um, it's great to sleep, you know, to be able to, to get to sleep, not have insomnia until of course I get the freaking asthma.
So I don't think that'll happen here. Cause I have like more fresh air and stuff. But anyways, that's my experience after a thousand miles, 977 miles of riding.
I'm not, I'm not in shape yet, but I can feel that I'm getting stronger. Uh, I'm at a higher level now than I was. Oh, and I'm out of time. Okay.
